jhu-lymphoma-scientific-research-mentori.why: The Lab/Translational Track is open to postdoctoral fellows up to 8 years post-PhD, so at ~7 years Eric clears the window that shuts him out of most 5-year-capped lymphoma fellowships. His T-cell lymphoma and computational focus fits the translational track, and eligibility runs on a valid US work visa rather than citizenship. It layers structured mentorship and a scholar grant on top of funding, useful for building lymphoma domain credibility.

cur-ash-scholar-award-fellow-fellow-to-f.why: PhD holders (not only MDs) are eligible, and at ~7 years post-PhD Eric maps onto the Fellow-to-Faculty level (6-11 years) built specifically for the postdoc-to-faculty transition he is in. Computational and outcomes projects on leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma all qualify, and visa or permanent-resident holders conducting research in the US are eligible. It requires ASH membership and a hematologic (not solid-tumor) focus, both of which suit his program.

cur-evansmds-young-investigator-award-ey.why: A transition-to-independence award for MDS researchers within ~9 years of their last graduate degree who are still in non-tenure-track positions, which is a clean fit for a ~7-years-out senior postdoc. There is no citizenship requirement (US nonprofit institution), and computational/ML work on MDS biology, genomics, or prognosis qualifies when paired with an established MDS mentor. Successful awardees also gain a fast track into the larger Discovery Research Grant.

cur-hanna-h-gray-fellows-program-howard-.why: A field-agnostic HHMI postdoc-to-faculty bridge open to non-US citizens at eligible US institutions like [redacted], designed to launch an independent lab, so a computational blood-cancer program is fully in scope. The key caveat is that it caps postdoctoral experience at 7 years, and at ~7 years Eric sits right at the eligibility boundary and should confirm his exact count. Prestige and the salary-plus-startup structure make it worth pursuing if he still qualifies.

cur-hope-funds-for-cancer-research-postd.why: Open to PhD holders (unlike the physician-only oncology awards), and the funded scope explicitly names rare leukemias, lymphomas, and MDS, matching his blood-cancer focus. Eligibility hinges on time in the current sponsor's lab (no more than ~2 years) rather than a hard post-PhD cap, so his ~7-years-out status is less of an obstacle than for most fellowships. No citizenship requirement, though the work must be at a US nonprofit institution.

cur-aamdsif-research-grants-program-apla.why: One of the few funders centered squarely on MDS and bone-marrow-failure syndromes, with a New Investigator track explicitly open to postdoctoral fellows and no stated citizenship restriction. It fits his interest in MDS/clonal-hematopoiesis modeling and bone-marrow transcriptomic analysis, and the two-year seed size suits a focused computational pilot. It requires an LOI and a project on aplastic anemia, MDS, or PNH.

cur-peer-reviewed-cancer-research-progra.why: The new Early Career Investigator Option targets researchers within 7 years of their last postdoctoral position with limited prior funding, and Blood, Lymphoma, and Myeloma are explicit topic areas, making this both a structural and thematic fit. There is no citizenship requirement and no preliminary data needed, which suits a novel ML concept in T-cell lymphoma or MDS. He should confirm whether the ECI option presumes an independent appointment before relying on it.

cur-mds-foundation-young-investigator-gr.why: Open to investigators within 10 years of their terminal degree who have not held an R01-level award, so Eric qualifies at ~7 years, and the scope explicitly includes molecular biology, cytogenetics, and prognosis, all amenable to ML. It is open to US or international investigators, so his citizenship is not a barrier. Note the requirement to be a dues-paying MDS Foundation professional member before the application will be reviewed.

cur-nlm-research-grants-advancing-bioinf.why: This R01 directly funds AI/ML methods for computational biology and omics, including single-cell and spatial data, mapping cleanly onto his cancer-genomics and multi-omics blood-cancer modeling. The PI need not be a US citizen and there is no early-stage-investigator restriction, so citizenship is not an obstacle. The main caveat is that it requires PI (faculty) status, making it a target for his transition off the postdoc.

cur-bone-marrow-failure-research-program.why: Directly targets his MDS/bone-marrow-failure interest, with no citizenship requirement and no preliminary data needed, which is ideal for a novel ML approach to BMF disease mechanisms or clonal-evolution modeling. The Early-Career Investigator track is written for newly independent faculty, so it fits his near-term trajectory more than his current postdoc status. Both foreign and domestic organizations are eligible.

cur-informatics-technology-for-cancer-re.why: The single most on-target NCI mechanism for a stats/ML lab building reusable cancer-informatics software, with RFAs that explicitly welcome novel ML/DL methods and an early-stage U01 suited to prototyping tools for leukemia/lymphoma/MDS data. There is no citizenship requirement for the PI. Because it needs an eligible PI appointment, it is most actionable as he moves toward independence rather than as a postdoc.

cur-mmrf-research-scholars-program-schol.why: This flagship early-career myeloma award is open to anyone holding no title higher than assistant professor at a US institution, which includes a senior postdoc, with no explicit citizenship gate. His multi-omic ML methods map directly onto MMRF's CoMMpass dataset for progression modeling from MGUS/smoldering myeloma. The scope is basic/translational myeloma, a step away from his primary T-cell lymphoma focus.

cur-new-investigator-research-grant-prog.why: Leukemia/MDS-specific and methodology-agnostic, so computational and AI/ML approaches qualify, and it is explicitly open to non-US citizens and international institutions, removing his citizenship concern. The caveat is that it requires holding an independent faculty appointment within 7 years of its start, so it is a target for his post-transition phase rather than his current postdoc. It is run by the Leukemia (not Lymphoma) Research Foundation.

cur-evansmds-discovery-research-grant-dr.why: The official RFA explicitly names machine learning for MDS and novel pathology/imaging algorithms as topics of interest, making it one of the most on-target computational-MDS grants available. The caveat is that it requires an independent faculty (assistant professor or above) appointment, so it is a forward-looking target as Eric transitions off the postdoc. Non-US investigators may apply subject to a US-equivalency determination.

cur-mds-research-fund-early-career-estab.why: The Early Career tier covers investigators within 10 years of their terminal degree with no prior R01 and only requires being based at a research institution, so a [redacted] senior postdoc plausibly qualifies. MDS-directed computational work (basic, translational, or clinical) fits, while AML/MPN-only projects are excluded. There is no stated citizenship barrier.

cur-bone-marrow-failure-research-program.why: Unusually for a DoD mechanism, it funds building datasets, computational tools, and shared infrastructure rather than a single hypothesis, a natural match for a data-science lab producing a curated multi-omic BMF/MDS resource or ML benchmark. There is no citizenship requirement. It is aimed at independent investigators, so it becomes most actionable once he is faculty.

cur-peer-reviewed-cancer-research-progra.why: A mentored career-development award pairing a newly independent early-career PI (within 7 years of their terminal degree) with a senior hematologic-oncology Career Guide who need not be at the same institution, with Blood/Lymphoma/Myeloma as qualifying topics and no citizenship requirement. This structure suits a stats/ML PI leaning on a clinical mentor. The caveat is that postdocs are explicitly ineligible, so it applies once Eric holds an independent appointment.

cur-ims-career-development-award-interna.why: A myeloma career-development award open to PhD holders who are not yet independent and have never received NIH funding, criteria that fit a senior postdoc without prior R01 support. There is no citizenship restriction for a US-based researcher and computational/ML myeloma projects are in scope, but it requires IMS membership and a myeloma (rather than lymphoma) focus. The salary support can directly fund his project.

cur-cri-immuno-informatics-postdoctoral-.why: Purpose-built for computational scientists deepening into cancer immunology with no citizenship restriction, an ideal thematic match for immune profiling of lymphoma. The significant caveat is that it requires fewer than 5 years of postdoctoral experience, so at ~7 years post-PhD this window has likely closed for him unless he can document a qualifying interruption. It also requires an explicit immunology angle to the computational work.

cur-ai2050-early-career-fellowship-schmi.why: An AI-for-science fellowship open to postdocs and pre-tenure researchers worldwide with no citizenship restriction, rewarding bold ML methods including biological and medical AI, which fits his computational-oncology methods program at his current stage. The caveat is that selection is by closed nomination, so he cannot self-apply and would need to cultivate nominators. Postdoc eligibility is what keeps it usable now rather than only post-transition.

cur-break-through-cancer-teamlabs-incl-a.why: Thematically ideal, combining an AML TeamLab with a dedicated Data Science Hub (generative-AI pathology, spatial transcriptomics, computational diagnostics) that matches his exact methods on blood cancer. Because [redacted]'s Koch Institute is a member institution, participation is realistically accessible through internal [redacted] channels rather than an open call. It is not an individual-applicant grant, so access depends on joining a team.

cur-pershing-square-sohn-prize-for-young.why: Now expanded to all US institutions, it explicitly welcomes technological and computational applications, requires no prior cancer-research pedigree, and allows non-US citizens with a valid visa, all well-matched to his methods-first profile. The caveat is that it requires 2-6 years of running an independent lab, so it is a target once he is a faculty PI rather than a postdoc. It would fund bold computational-oncology work on blood cancers.

cur-nih-director-s-new-innovator-award-d.why: A High-Risk/High-Reward award rewarding bold, unconventional ML methods with no preliminary data or disease-specific framing required, ideal for launching an AI-for-blood-cancer lab. Eligibility runs through Early Stage Investigator status (within 10 years of the terminal degree) and requires an independent US position, so it is a first-year-faculty target. There is no stated citizenship requirement, unlike the NIH K awards.

cur-mmrf-research-fellowship-program-mul.why: A lower-barrier, one-year mentored myeloma fellowship for postdocs working under a qualified myeloma mentor, a realistic entry point for Eric to fund a computational myeloma project at his current stage. It is less aligned with his core T-cell lymphoma interest and requires pairing with a myeloma-focused mentor. Current terms should be verified on the RFA.

cur-single-cell-biology-data-insights-rf.why: Squarely in his wheelhouse, funding computational/ML methods for large single-cell datasets applicable to leukemia/lymphoma/MDS and bone-marrow atlases, and the RFA explicitly welcomes computational experts and foreign institutions. The caveat is that the most recent cycle series has closed, so this is one to monitor for a successor rather than apply to today. Early-career investigators are strongly encouraged when it runs.

jhu-hollis-brownstein-research-grants-pr.why: A blood-cancer New Investigator grant open to independent faculty within 7 years of their first appointment, with no citizenship requirement and projects from outside the US eligible, so his status is not a barrier. His leukemia/MDS computational work fits, but eligibility requires the faculty position he does not yet hold, making it a post-transition target. It is a two-stage LOI/full-application program on ProposalCentral.

cur-damon-runyon-rachleff-innovation-awa.why: Supports high-risk/high-reward ideas lacking preliminary data across all scientific disciplines, so a novel ML approach to blood cancers qualifies, and non-US citizens are eligible if research is conducted at a US institution. The caveat is that it requires a tenure-track assistant professor appointment in the first 5 years, making it a post-transition target rather than a postdoc mechanism. Its methods-development framing fits his profile well.

cur-mds-pilot-research-grants-the-taub-f.why: A high-risk/high-reward MDS pilot intentionally structured to fund bold ideas without preliminary data, which is friendly to a novel computational method. Neither citizenship nor a visa is required, but it needs a faculty position at instructor level or above, so it applies once he is independent. Clinical studies and AML/MPN-focused projects are excluded.

jhu-career-development-program-special-f.why: A senior-postdoc transition award open to non-US citizens at institutions worldwide, with research needing only to be relevant to hematologic malignancies, so ML applied to leukemia/lymphoma fits. The caveat is that it targets postdocs with 3-5 years of training, so at ~7 years post-PhD Eric may be past the window and should confirm his eligibility. It provides stipend support aimed at those closest to independence.

cur-aacr-hematologic-malignancies-resear.why: The single best-targeted blood-cancer postdoc fellowship, dedicated exclusively to leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS, with computational/AI projects qualifying as basic or translational and only a legal right to work required rather than citizenship. The caveat is that it is limited to fellows within 5 years of the doctoral degree, so at ~7 years post-PhD this window has likely closed for him. It also requires AACR membership.

cur-career-awards-at-the-scientific-inte.why: Thematically the most tailored award available, purpose-built for computational/quantitative scientists transitioning into biomedical research and open to visa holders at US institutions. The serious caveat is that it caps postdoctoral experience at 60 months, and at ~7 years (roughly 84 months) post-PhD Eric has almost certainly aged out of the window. It is included because the intent matches his profile exactly, even though eligibility is the obstacle.

cur-mentored-quantitative-research-caree.why: The NIH mechanism designed precisely for a quantitative/ML scientist pivoting into biomedical research, where limited prior cancer-biology experience is an expected feature rather than a weakness, making it a conceptually ideal match. Two caveats limit it: it requires US citizenship or permanent residency (his [redacted] is expected only [redacted]) and a mentored faculty appointment. NCI or NHLBI would be the natural institutes for his blood-cancer work once he is eligible.

jhu-clinical-investigator-career-develop.why: This is almost purpose-built for Maria: an LRF career-development award for licensed physicians at the advanced-fellow or junior-faculty stage doing patient-based lymphoma research, which is exactly her DLBCL CAR-T translational program. Her U.S. citizenship and ACGME hem/onc training satisfy the eligibility, and her 80% research effort comfortably clears the 35-50% minimum. One reserved award targets underrepresented patient populations, which her lymphoma-clinic population may speak to.

jhu-damon-runyon-physician-scientist-tra.why: Maria hits nearly every criterion: an MD (not MD/PhD), board-eligible, having completed clinical training, committing at least 80% research effort. The award is designed to recruit exactly her kind of clinician into a cancer research career, and DLBCL CAR-T immunotherapy is squarely in scope. The main caveat is that she must apply before starting an initial assistant professorship and have no more than three years of postdoctoral research experience.

jhu-damon-runyon-fellowship-award-damon-.why: The physician-scientist track of this fellowship keys off completing clinical training and being board-eligible with at least 80% research time, all of which describe Maria, rather than a strict degree-age limit. It supports mentored cancer research and offers medical-school loan repayment for eligible physician-scientists. It is best suited if she is currently in a mentored research-fellow phase rather than already independent faculty.

cur-ash-scholar-award-fellow-fellow-to-f.why: ASH's flagship transition award has a Fellow-to-Faculty and Junior Faculty level that maps directly onto Maria's post-fellowship, board-eligible stage, and it emphasizes hematologic malignancies including patient-oriented and translational lymphoma work. Her CAR-T/DLBCL focus is a strong hematologic fit and her heavy research effort suits the protected-time expectation. She would need to hold ASH membership in good standing by the LOI.

jhu-career-development-award-american-so.why: Conquer Cancer's CDA funds physicians in the first four years of a faculty appointment in a clinical department who are launching an independent clinical cancer research program, which fits Maria if she has just moved into junior faculty. Her lymphoma-clinic base and CAR-T translational agenda are a natural clinical-oncology fit, and her active medical licensure/board-eligibility meets the clinical requirement. The caveat is that it requires an actual faculty appointment, so it applies once she is on faculty rather than still a fellow.

jhu-young-investigator-award-conquer-can.why: This Conquer Cancer YIA funds physicians transitioning from fellowship to a faculty appointment to pursue clinical oncology research, matching Maria's current inflection point with her DLBCL CAR-T work. It requires ASCO membership and a mentor, both readily arranged in an academic lymphoma program. The eligibility window (within about two years of finishing subspecialty training and within ten years of the MD) is a caveat, since her 2016 MD puts her right at the ten-year boundary.

jhu-young-investigator-award-yia-america.why: The same Conquer Cancer YIA family supports early-career physician-investigators in clinical oncology moving from fellowship toward faculty, which fits her clinical-translational lymphoma trajectory. Her active medical license and mentored academic setting satisfy the core requirements. The within-ten-years-of-medical-degree cutoff is a real caveat given she earned her MD in 2016, though a foreign-medical-graduate-style exception does not apply to her.

jhu-aacr-conquer-cancer-foundation-of-as.why: This co-sponsored award funds a physician-scientist during the fellowship-to-faculty transition specifically to promote translational oncology, which describes Maria's bench-to-clinic CAR-T resistance work in DLBCL. It requires at least 60% research effort (she has 80%) and AACR membership. The caveat is the eligibility window of the final two years of subspecialty training and within ten years of the medical degree, which her recently completed fellowship and 2016 MD place at the margin.

jhu-lymphoma-scientific-research-mentori.why: The LSRMP Clinical Track is explicitly for clinical fellows or junior faculty (Instructor/Assistant Professor within five years) building independent lymphoma projects, which is precisely Maria's stage and disease focus. It pairs a small scholar grant with structured mentorship, ideal for someone establishing a CAR-T/DLBCL research identity. Her U.S. citizenship and U.S. institution satisfy the residency conditions.

cur-lrf-lymphoma-scientific-research-men.why: This LRF mentoring program combines a research grant with senior mentorship for early-career lymphoma investigators, and its named scholar tracks (including CLL/SLL and follicular lymphoma) sit within her hematologic-malignancy wheelhouse. As a board-eligible clinician entering independent DLBCL research, she fits the clinical/junior-faculty track and would benefit from the domain mentorship. Her citizenship and U.S. institution meet the work-authorization requirement.

jhu-postdoctoral-fellowship-lymphoma-res.why: LRF's fellowship supports clinical fellows doing lymphoma research with a minimum 80% research commitment, which matches both her effort level and her DLBCL focus. Clinical fellows are eligible if they have completed roughly two to six years of fellowship, so timing is favorable if she recently finished. Special initiatives reserve awards for follicular and mantle cell lymphoma and for underrepresented applicants, and it is best suited if she is in a research-fellow rather than faculty role.

cur-lrf-postdoctoral-fellowship-grant-ly.why: This LRF postdoctoral grant funds basic, translational, or clinical lymphoma research and is open to MD fellows (typically two to five years of fellowship), covering her CAR-T resistance work in DLBCL. It reserves awards for follicular and mantle cell lymphoma and for underrepresented applicants. The fit depends on her being within the fellow-stage window rather than already appointed as faculty.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-schol.why: The LLS Scholar in Clinical Research tier is built for faculty-level investigators leading patient-facing blood cancer research such as early-phase trials, which aligns tightly with Maria's clinical lymphoma practice and translational CAR-T program. Its five-year horizon suits building an independent DLBCL clinical-research line. It requires an independent tenure-track (or equivalent) appointment, so it fits once she is faculty rather than a fellow.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-fello.why: The LLS CDP Fellow tier supports early postdoctoral researchers (within about three years of training) in mentored blood-cancer research, and DLBCL/CAR-T work is directly relevant to hematologic malignancies. As an MD in a mentored research phase she qualifies, and the program is open to U.S. citizens at nonprofit institutions. It is the right entry tier only if she is still early in mentored research rather than independent.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-speci.why: The Special Fellow tier targets more advanced postdocs or instructors (roughly three to five years of research training) needing additional mentored time before independence, which fits a board-eligible physician-scientist finishing her research runway. Her CAR-T/DLBCL project clearly meets the hematologic-malignancy relevance requirement. It applies while she is still in a mentored, non-independent position.

jhu-career-development-program-special-f.why: This is the same LLS (now Blood Cancer United) Special Fellow mechanism for later-stage postdocs/instructors doing hematologic-malignancy research, which covers her lymphoma CAR-T work. She meets the MD-degree and mentored-position criteria, and it is open to applicants regardless of citizenship at nonprofit institutions. It suits the transitional research phase just before she competes for independence.

cur-mentored-clinical-scientist-career-d.why: As a U.S. citizen MD with ~80% research effort, Maria cleanly meets the citizenship and 75%-effort requirements, and the K23 companion is tailored to patient-oriented research like clinical CAR-T outcomes in DLBCL while the K08 covers translational resistance-mechanism work. NCI participates, matching her oncology focus. This is one of the strongest federal career-development fits for a clinically active physician-scientist not yet at associate professor.

cur-mentored-clinical-scientist-research.why: The standalone K08 gives clinically trained MDs protected, mentored time for laboratory or translational research, well matched to Maria's mechanistic work on immunotherapy resistance in DLBCL. She satisfies the clinical-doctorate, U.S.-citizenship, and 75%-effort requirements and is not yet at associate professor. It is oriented to non-patient-oriented (lab/translational) aims, so a purely clinical-trial project would instead route to the K23.

cur-doris-duke-physician-scientist-fello.why: This fellowship gives subspecialty clinical fellows additional protected research years, and Maria's completed hematology/oncology fellowship and DLBCL research focus fit its intent well. It is aimed at MDs in or recently out of subspecialty training. The important caveat is that faculty and assistant professors are explicitly ineligible, so it fits only if she has not yet taken a faculty appointment.

cur-doris-duke-clinical-scientist-develo.why: The Doris Duke CSDA funds early-career physician-scientists at the assistant-professor level transitioning to independent clinical research, fitting Maria's patient-oriented CAR-T/DLBCL agenda once she is faculty. Her active clinical role and MD meet the core criteria. Note the will-based restriction against animal work, which is not limiting for her human-subjects clinical research but confines projects to patient/clinical data.

cur-clinician-scientist-development-gran.why: The ACS CSDG helps full-time faculty clinician-scientists who maintain clinical practice become independent investigators, which describes Maria's dual clinical-and-research role in a lymphoma clinic. Her MD, active clinical involvement, and translational CAR-T focus fit its intent, with eligibility tied to a U.S. institution rather than citizenship (which she has). It requires that she not already lead a major independent grant, matching an early-career applicant.

cur-aacr-astrazeneca-career-development-.why: This award is dedicated specifically to early-career physician-scientists doing cancer research, and its open disease scope readily accommodates her clinical-translational DLBCL immunotherapy work. As a board-eligible MD building an independent program she fits the intended stage, and AACR membership is straightforward to obtain. Exact eligibility windows should be confirmed on the AACR page, but the physician-scientist framing is a direct match.

jhu-medical-research-award-gabrielle-s-a.why: Gabrielle's Angel funds early-career blood-cancer research in leukemia and lymphoma with near-term clinical-trial potential, which is exactly the translational thrust of her CAR-T resistance work in DLBCL. Assistant-professor-stage investigators are the target, fitting her transition. The key caveat is that it is invitation-only and limited to NCI-designated cancer centers, so she would need her institution to nominate her.

cur-gabrielle-s-angel-foundation-medical.why: The same Gabrielle's Angel award emphasizes translational leukemia/lymphoma research translatable to clinical trials within a few years, aligning with her DLBCL CAR-T program at the assistant-professor stage. Her hematologic-malignancy focus is a clean thematic fit. It remains invitation-only through NCI-designated institutions and does not accept international proposals, so nomination through a designated cancer center is the gating step.

cur-peer-reviewed-cancer-research-progra.why: The DoD PRCRP names Lymphoma as an explicit topic area and requires no preliminary data, making it a strong general fit for a novel DLBCL CAR-T resistance idea. The new Early Career Investigator Option (within about seven years of clinical fellowship) suits her recently completed hem/onc training. There is no citizenship barrier, and the application simply must address a required PRCRP topic that lymphoma satisfies.

This PRCRP mechanism gives newly independent early-career investigators (within about seven years of terminal degree/training) multi-year support under a senior Career Guide, in topic areas that include Lymphoma, fitting her DLBCL research plan. She would pair with an experienced hematologic-oncology mentor, which suits her clinical-translational stage. The caveat is that postdocs are ineligible and she must hold independent lab space, so it fits once she is faculty.

jhu-aacr-gertrude-b-elion-cancer-researc.why: This AACR award supports early-career faculty in clinical/translational oncology within the first six years of a first independent faculty position, a good match once Maria is an assistant professor pursuing her DLBCL immunotherapy program. It carries no citizenship restriction and requires AACR membership. The clear caveat is that it requires an assistant-professor appointment and excludes those still in fellowship or already at associate rank.

cur-aacr-hematologic-malignancies-resear.why: This is AACR's fellowship dedicated exclusively to blood cancers, so her lymphoma/CAR-T translational work is thematically ideal and clinical research fellows are eligible. The significant caveat is the requirement to be within five years of the most recent doctoral degree; with a 2016 MD she likely falls outside that window unless a training-based interpretation applies, so eligibility should be confirmed before investing effort.

jhu-aacr-immuno-oncology-research-fellow.why: CAR-T and immunotherapy resistance sit at the center of immuno-oncology, so the scientific fit with this AACR fellowship is excellent and clinical fellows are eligible. It requires AACR membership and has no citizenship barrier. The main caveat is the eligibility window of completing the doctoral degree within the past five years, which her 2016 MD may exceed, so she should verify whether her clinical-training timeline still qualifies her.

jhu-postdoctoral-fellowship-hope-funds-f.why: Hope Funds supports postdoctoral MDs proposing innovative work on difficult-to-treat cancers, and immunotherapy-resistant DLBCL is a fitting hard-to-treat target for her project. It accepts MDs and requires research at a U.S. nonprofit institution, which her U.S. setting satisfies. It fits best if she is in a sponsored postdoctoral phase (within about two years in the sponsor's lab) rather than already independent faculty.

jhu-hollis-brownstein-research-grants-pr.why: This Leukemia Research Foundation New Investigator grant funds independent investigators within seven years of a first faculty appointment studying blood cancers, a category that includes her lymphoma work. There is no citizenship requirement and it is a useful early-independence seed grant. The caveat is that it requires an independent faculty-level appointment and the foundation's emphasis leans toward leukemia, so her DLBCL project would need to be framed within its broader blood-cancer scope.

cur-lrf-clinical-investigator-career-dev.why: The same LRF physician-investigator mechanism, providing salary support for a licensed clinician transitioning to independent lymphoma research, which fits her CAR-T resistance work in DLBCL. It requires clinical observation of human subjects, matching her lymphoma-clinic patient base, and her board-eligibility in an ACGME specialty meets the licensure criterion. Being at a U.S. institution as a citizen fully satisfies the residency requirement.

cur-damon-runyon-quantitative-biology-fe.why: This fellowship is purpose-built to bring math/CS/ML-trained postdocs into cancer research under joint computational ('dry') and cancer-biology ('wet') mentorship, which maps almost exactly onto Jun's ML/deep-learning work on single-cell RNA-seq of AML. Eligibility (PhD within 5 years, under 24 months in the mentor's lab) fits a 2nd-year postdoc, and there is no citizenship requirement as long as the work is at a US institution, so his non-US status is not a barrier. The dual-mentor structure would push him to pair with an AML/hematology lab, strengthening the blood-cancer framing.

cur-aacr-hematologic-malignancies-resear.why: This is AACR's fellowship dedicated exclusively to blood cancers, directly funding leukemia work, and computational/AI methodology qualifies under its basic/translational scope, so an ML-on-AML-scRNA-seq project sits squarely in remit. Jun is within the 5-years-post-PhD window and, as a non-US citizen with legal right to work in the US, meets the eligibility; he would need to hold AACR membership. The only caveat is that US-government and for-profit employees are excluded, which does not affect an academic postdoc.

cur-nih-pathway-to-independence-award-k9.why: K99/R00 is the flagship postdoc-to-faculty transition award and, unusually for NIH, carries no citizenship requirement, so Jun's non-US status is not disqualifying while his ~2-years-post-PhD timing sits comfortably inside the early-stage postdoc window. His AI/computational-oncology program on AML is a natural NCI fit, and applying now (2nd/3rd postdoc year) maximizes the benefit of the mentored K99 phase. A blood-cancer mentor and a clear independence plan would make the strongest case.

jhu-nci-pathway-to-independence-award-fo.why: This is the specific NCI reissue (PAR-25-135) of the K99/R00 for cancer-focused early-stage postdocs, and its clinical-trial-not-allowed design fits a purely computational AML project that involves no trial. It explicitly welcomes non-US citizens with appropriate visa status, matching Jun's situation, and his single-cell AML methods align with NCI's mission. It overlaps with the general K99/R00 entry, so he would pursue one submission through NCI rather than both.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-fello.why: The LLS/Blood Cancer United Fellow tier is the entry mechanism for a within-3-years postdoc whose research need only be 'relevant to' hematologic malignancies, so ML applied to AML single-cell data clearly qualifies. It is explicitly open to non-US citizens at nonprofit institutions worldwide, removing the citizenship barrier. Because LLS has no dedicated computational track, Jun would want an AML/hematology-connected mentor to anchor the blood-cancer relevance.

cur-postdoctoral-fellowship-pf-american-.why: ACS explicitly funds AI/computational and in-silico methods across the cancer continuum, so a machine-learning AML project is fully in scope, and Jun meets the criteria (terminal degree and postdoc training both within 3 years). Its citizenship rules accommodate international postdocs holding an appropriate visa, so his non-US status is not disqualifying. This is the most directly applicable ACS mechanism for his current stage.

cur-hanna-h-gray-fellows-program-howard-.why: HHMI's field-agnostic postdoc-to-faculty bridge accepts any area of basic biomedical research, so an AI/ML computational blood-cancer program qualifies, and it is open regardless of background including non-US postdocs at eligible US institutions. Jun is well within the 7-years-postdoc limit and its faculty-search support directly serves his transition goal. It is highly competitive, so a distinctive AML methods program would need to stand out on its basic-science framing.

cur-lsrf-postdoctoral-fellowship-life-sc.why: This broad, prestigious life-sciences fellowship covers computational biology and cancer with no field restriction, so Jun's ML-on-AML work qualifies, and it is explicitly non-citizen-friendly (foreign nationals hold it at US labs). He is within the 5-years-post-PhD eligibility, and the only structural requirement is that the postdoc lab differ from his thesis lab, which is standard for a 2nd-year postdoc.

cur-ash-scholar-award-fellow-fellow-to-f.why: ASH's Scholar Award admits PhD (not just MD) applicants and funds computational hematology, so Jun's AML modeling fits, and the visa/permanent-resident-holder pathway (research conducted in the US) accommodates his status. At ~2 years post-PhD he lands in the Fellow level, though its typical 3-5 years of research experience means he is at the early edge of that window. ASH membership and a strictly hematologic (not solid-tumor) focus are required, both easily met.

cur-break-through-cancer-teamlabs-incl-a.why: This is a near-perfect thematic match, combining a dedicated AML TeamLab with a Data Science Hub doing computational diagnostics on single-cell and spatial data, exactly Jun's methods-and-disease intersection. Access is through the five member institutions, and [redacted] (Koch Institute) is one, so participation is realistically reachable via internal channels rather than a cold application. It is not an open individual grant, so this is a collaboration route rather than a PI award for a postdoc.

cur-single-cell-biology-data-insights-rf.why: CZI's RFA funds computational methods and ML that extract insight from large single-cell datasets and explicitly welcomes computational experts, which is precisely Jun's single-cell RNA-seq AML modeling profile, with no citizenship barrier (foreign nonprofit PIs allowed). The main caveat is timing: the most recent cycle has closed and a successor is not guaranteed, so this is a watch-for-reopening opportunity. If revived, his profile would be squarely in the target zone.

cur-mentored-quantitative-research-caree.why: The K25 is purpose-built to move quantitative scientists (statistics/CS/ML) into biomedical research, so a limited prior cancer-biology background is an expected feature rather than a weakness, making it arguably the single best-tailored NIH mechanism for Jun's pivot into computational oncology. The binding caveat is that it requires US citizenship or permanent residency at time of award; with PR expected ~2027, a submission timed to a 2027 award could become plausibly eligible, but this is not guaranteed. NCI or NHLBI would be the natural institutes for AML work.

jhu-immuno-informatics-postdoctoral-fell.why: This CRI fellowship is designed for postdocs building dual expertise in immunology and data science, which fits Jun's AI/computational skillset if his AML single-cell work is framed around the leukemic immune microenvironment. It shares CRI's no-citizenship-restriction framework, so his non-US status is not a barrier. Fit hinges on an immunology angle; a purely genomic (non-immune) AML project would be a weaker match to the immuno-informatics scope.

cur-aacr-anna-d-barker-basic-cancer-rese.why: This AACR postdoctoral fellowship supports mentored basic cancer research and accommodates quantitative/computational-biology projects, so ML on AML biology qualifies when framed as basic science. It requires AACR membership but has no citizenship restriction relevant to a US-based postdoc. It is a solid general-purpose backstop to the more targeted hematologic-malignancies fellowship.

jhu-aacr-immuno-oncology-research-fellow.why: This fellowship funds mentored immuno-oncology across basic and translational science for postdocs within 5 years of the doctorate, and Jun qualifies on stage with no citizenship requirement (AACR membership needed). It fits if his AML scRNA-seq work is oriented toward immune-cell states or the tumor immune microenvironment. A non-immunology framing of his AML modeling would fit the program less well.

jhu-cri-irvington-postdoctoral-fellowshi.why: CRI's flagship postdoc fellowship funds tumor/cancer immunology under a qualified sponsor with no citizenship restriction and a under-5-years-postdoc window, both compatible with Jun. It is a strong option only if his AML single-cell analysis centers on immunology (immune cell populations, anti-leukemic immunity); a purely non-immune computational project would fall outside the immunology remit.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-speci.why: The Special Fellow tier targets more advanced postdocs (roughly 3-5 years) needing extra mentored time before independence, and like the Fellow tier it is open worldwide to non-US citizens with hematologic-malignancy-relevant research, so his AML ML work qualifies. At ~2 years post-PhD Jun is slightly early for this tier and would more naturally fit the Fellow level now, making this a strong option a year or two later. Same requirement for an AML/hematology-anchored project applies.

jhu-postdoctoral-fellowship-hope-funds-f.why: Hope Funds supports innovative postdoctoral work on difficult-to-treat cancers with no citizenship requirement provided the research is at a US nonprofit institution, and AML fits its blood-cancer/oncology scope. Jun's early-postdoc timing works because the 2-year-in-sponsor's-lab rule applies only if already appointed. The competition emphasizes highly innovative science, so a distinctive ML methods angle on AML would strengthen the case.

cur-ruth-l-kirschstein-nrsa-individual-p.why: The F32 supports mentored postdoctoral training and NCI funds computational/quantitative cancer projects, so an AML ML project with a defined cancer focus is fundable and it pairs well as a stepping stone toward a K99/R00. The decisive caveat is the statutory NRSA requirement of US citizenship, national status, or permanent residency at time of award; Jun is currently ineligible but could qualify if his PR (expected ~2027) is in hand before an award. This makes it a plausible future rather than immediate option.

cur-mentored-research-scientist-developm.why: The K01 offers protected mentored time for PhD scientists building an independent program and many institutes use it for computational skill development, so it suits Jun's oncology pivot, though the K25 is better tailored when prior biomedical experience is limited. Like other NIH career awards it requires US citizenship or permanent residency at time of award, so eligibility depends on his PR coming through (~2027) before an award is made. It is best viewed as a contingent, later-stage option.

jhu-proposals-in-leukemia-research-when-.why: This RFA solicits laboratory, translational, or clinical research on acute leukemia and is open to all career stages with no stated citizenship requirement, making it one of the most on-topic AML-specific options for Jun's disease focus. The practical caveat is that it is oriented toward investigators running their own projects, so a postdoc would typically apply through or alongside a mentor/PI. The one-year renewable structure suits a focused computational AML aim.

cur-leukemia-exploration-and-prevention-.why: LEAP is a blood-cancer RFA centered on RUNX1-FPD and associated hematologic malignancies, particularly AML, and it explicitly welcomes computational and systems approaches, aligning tightly with Jun's AML methods. The major caveat is eligibility: it targets full-time independent investigators, so as a postdoc without an independent faculty appointment he is likely not yet eligible and would revisit this after transition. The scientific scope is also narrow (RUNX1 predisposition) relative to general AML.

jhu-clra-research-grants-children-s-leuk.why: This recurring grant funds research into the causes and cures of leukemia with no explicit career-stage restriction, so it is thematically aligned with Jun's AML focus and could support a computational aim. Research must be US-based, which fits his [redacted]-area setting, and no citizenship requirement is stated. The modest award size and typical orientation toward a supervising investigator mean a postdoc would likely apply with mentor support.

cur-lrf-postdoctoral-fellowship-grant-ly.why: This lymphoma fellowship is methodology-agnostic and open to PhD-trained (non-MD) postdocs within 5 years of the degree, with a US/Canada work-visa pathway that fits Jun's status, so a computational classifier or outcome-prediction project would qualify. The caveat is disease fit: it funds lymphoma rather than AML, so he would need to reorient onto a lymphoid malignancy to be competitive. It is a good adjacent option if his methods generalize beyond myeloid disease.

cur-acs-flatiron-health-real-world-data-.why: This ACS+Flatiron award is explicitly data-science-focused, leveraging real-world oncology data, so it fits an AI/ML researcher and could accommodate hematologic-malignancy questions if Jun works with EHR-derived datasets. Two caveats temper the fit: his core expertise is single-cell RNA-seq rather than real-world clinical data, and applicants are generally expected to already be within the ACS research ecosystem, which may gate a first-time applicant. It is a stretch option contingent on a real-world-data angle.

cur-mmrf-research-fellowship-program-mul.why: This one-year MMRF fellowship supports postdocs doing mentored myeloma research and is a lower-barrier entry point where a computational project would fit its data-rich (e.g., CoMMpass) environment. The principal caveat is disease mismatch: it funds multiple myeloma, not AML, so Jun would need to redirect his single-cell modeling onto myeloma to be eligible in spirit. Best considered only if he is open to a plasma-cell-malignancy application of his methods.

jhu-damon-runyon-fellowship-award-damon-.why: This prestigious postdoctoral fellowship funds basic and physician-scientists in cancer research with no citizenship requirement (research at a US institution), and computational cancer projects are eligible, so the science fits Jun's AML work. The binding caveat is timing: the basic-scientist track expects the PhD within roughly 18 months of the deadline and under one year in the sponsor's lab, and as a 2nd-year postdoc who earned his PhD in 2024 he is likely past that window. The more open-ended Damon Runyon Quantitative Biology Fellowship is the better-matched sibling program for him.

cur-rally-foundation-research-grants-pos.why: Rally offers a postdoctoral-fellow grant tier and explicitly encourages data-utilization and innovative approaches, so an ML/computational project would be welcomed and Jun's PhD-postdoc stage is eligible. The key constraint is that research must target childhood/pediatric cancer, so this fits only if his AML work addresses pediatric AML rather than adult disease. It is a viable option if he can frame a pediatric-leukemia application of his single-cell methods.

cur-mmrf-research-scholars-program-schol.why: This flagship MMRF award funds basic/translational myeloma research and its eligibility caps applicants at assistant-professor level, which technically admits a postdoc, and its multi-omic CoMMpass data is well suited to ML modeling. Two caveats limit fit: it is primarily an early-independent-investigator award more naturally held by junior faculty, and it funds myeloma rather than Jun's AML focus. He would need both a myeloma reorientation and a strong independence case to compete.

cur-ruth-l-kirschstein-nrsa-institutiona.why: A T32 slot in a computational-biology, cancer, or hematology training program would provide NRSA stipend support and structured training aligned with Jun's AML methods, and [redacted]/Harvard/Broad host relevant programs he could seek appointment to. The decisive caveat is the statutory NRSA citizenship/permanent-residency requirement at appointment, so he is not currently eligible and could only be appointed once his PR (expected ~2027) is granted. It is an institutional appointment rather than something he applies for directly to NIH.

cur-jane-coffin-childs-postdoctoral-fell.why: The Fund's cancer/medical-research mission and openness to computational projects and non-US citizens at US institutions make the science and status a fit for Jun's blood-cancer ML work. The blocking caveat is the early-career window: it requires the PhD within about 24 months and no more than 12 months of prior postdoc experience, and as a 2nd-year postdoc he has likely aged out of eligibility. It would only be viable if his exact PhD date and lab-start timing still fall inside those limits.

cur-helen-hay-whitney-foundation-postdoc.why: This foundation funds all basic biomedical science including computational blood-cancer research and allows foreign citizens to hold it in US labs, so the disease and citizenship dimensions fit Jun. The limiting caveat is that it is designed for very early postdocs, requiring the PhD within roughly two years and no more than one year in the postdoc lab, and a 2nd-year postdoc almost certainly exceeds the one-year-in-lab limit. It is effectively a missed-window option unless his timing is unusually recent.
